_meta.authoringNotes: Epidemiology per Radhakrishnan K et al. Brain 1994 population study (PMID 8004085) — incidence approximately 83 per 100,000 per year. Clinical features and natural history per Iyer S, Kim HJ. Curr Rev Musculoskelet Med 2016 (PMID 27250042) and Caridi JM et al. HSS J 2011 (PMID 23024623). Diagnosis and Spurling test per Wainner RS et al. Spine 2003 (PMID 12544929). Management per the North American Spine Society (NASS) clinical guideline on cervical radiculopathy from degenerative disorders (2010, updated) and Bono CM et al. Spine J 2011 (PMID 21168100). Surgical outcomes (ACDF vs disc replacement) per the FDA IDE trials and long-term cervical disc arthroplasty data. Conservative care and natural-history recovery per Wong JJ et al. Spine J 2014.

heroOverview: Cervical radiculopathy is the set of symptoms that arise when a nerve root in the neck is compressed or irritated as it exits the spinal canal, producing pain, numbness, tingling, and sometimes weakness that radiate from the neck into the shoulder, arm, and hand. The population incidence is roughly 83 per 100,000 people per year, with a peak in the fifth and sixth decades. The two main causes are cervical disc herniation, more common in younger and middle-aged adults, and degenerative narrowing of the bony channel the nerve passes through (foraminal stenosis from spondylosis), more common in older adults. The pattern of symptoms maps to the affected nerve root — the C6 and C7 roots are the most commonly involved, producing characteristic patterns of arm and hand symptoms. The encouraging fact about cervical radiculopathy is that it has a favourable natural history: the majority of people improve substantially with conservative treatment — relative rest, analgesia, physiotherapy, and time — within weeks to a few months, and surgery is reserved for those with persistent disabling symptoms, significant or progressive weakness, or signs of spinal cord involvement. The key clinical task is to relieve symptoms and restore function while watching for the red flags that change management.

keyStats.prevalence: Annual incidence approximately 83 per 100,000 people (Radhakrishnan Brain 1994); higher in adults aged 50-60
keyStats.demographics: Slightly more common in men; peak incidence in the fifth and sixth decades
keyStats.avgAge: Most common between ages 45 and 65; disc herniation tends to cause it in younger adults, degenerative stenosis in older adults
keyStats.globalCases: One of the most common causes of neck-and-arm pain seen in primary care, spine, and rehabilitation services worldwide

definition: Cervical radiculopathy (ICD-10: M50.1 for cervical disc disorder with radiculopathy; M54.12-M54.13 for radiculopathy of the cervical and cervicothoracic region) is a clinical syndrome caused by dysfunction of a cervical nerve root, producing neurological symptoms — pain, sensory disturbance, and motor weakness — in the distribution of that root. It results from mechanical compression and chemical irritation of the nerve root within the intervertebral foramen, the bony channel through which the root exits the cervical spine. The two principal structural causes are a herniated cervical intervertebral disc, in which disc material protrudes and presses on the adjacent nerve root, and cervical spondylosis, the age-related degeneration of the discs, facet joints, and vertebrae that produces bony spurs (osteophytes) and narrowing of the foramen (foraminal stenosis). Less common causes include tumors, infection, and inflammatory or vascular conditions affecting the nerve root. The C7 nerve root (from the C6-C7 level) is the most commonly affected, followed by C6, and each root has a characteristic pattern of pain radiation, sensory loss, weakness, and reflex change. Cervical radiculopathy must be distinguished from cervical myelopathy, which is compression of the spinal cord itself rather than a nerve root and is a more serious condition, and from peripheral causes of arm symptoms such as carpal tunnel syndrome. It is managed by physical medicine and rehabilitation, primary care, neurology, pain medicine, and spinal surgery, with most cases managed without surgery.

typesClassification.description: Caused by a soft cervical disc herniation compressing the nerve root, more common in younger and middle-aged adults. It often has a more acute onset and a particularly favourable natural history, as herniated disc material frequently resorbs over time.

Caused by age-related degeneration — osteophytes, disc-space narrowing, and facet hypertrophy — producing foraminal stenosis. More common in older adults, often with a more gradual onset and a tendency to be more persistent than disc-related radiculopathy.
Affects the C5 root, causing pain and sensory change over the shoulder and upper arm, weakness of shoulder abduction (deltoid), and a reduced biceps reflex.

One of the two most common patterns. Causes pain and sensory symptoms down the lateral forearm into the thumb and index finger, weakness of elbow flexion and wrist extension, and a reduced biceps or brachioradialis reflex.
The single most common pattern. Causes pain and sensory symptoms into the middle finger, weakness of elbow extension (triceps) and wrist flexion, and a reduced triceps reflex.
Less common; affects the medial forearm and hand, with weakness of the intrinsic hand muscles and finger flexors, and can be confused with ulnar nerve problems.

primarySymptoms: Pain radiating from the neck into the shoulder, arm, and hand in a pattern that follows the affected nerve root, often sharp, burning, or electric in quality.
Neck pain and stiffness, which may be less prominent than the arm pain or, in some people, absent altogether.
Numbness and tingling (paresthesia) in a specific area of the arm, forearm, or hand corresponding to the affected nerve root's sensory distribution.
Weakness in particular arm or hand muscles supplied by the affected root — for example difficulty straightening the elbow in C7 radiculopathy or lifting the arm in C5 radiculopathy.
Symptoms that are typically worse with certain neck positions, particularly extending the neck or turning and tilting the head toward the affected side.
Relief of arm symptoms when the arm is raised and the hand placed on top of the head — the shoulder abduction relief sign.
Worsening of symptoms with coughing, sneezing, or straining, which transiently increases pressure on the nerve root.
A dull ache around the shoulder blade or between the shoulder blades on the affected side.
Difficulty with fine hand tasks or grip when motor weakness is present.
Disturbed sleep because of arm pain, and difficulty finding a comfortable neck and arm position at night.
Reduced or absent tendon reflexes in the arm corresponding to the affected nerve root.
earlyWarningSigns: New pain radiating from the neck down one arm in a band-like or finger-specific pattern
Tingling or numbness in specific fingers rather than the whole hand
Arm symptoms that change with neck position or are eased by resting the hand on top of the head
Difficulty with a specific arm movement, such as straightening the elbow or gripping
Neck-and-arm symptoms triggered or worsened by coughing, sneezing, or straining
emergencySigns: Weakness, numbness, or clumsiness affecting both arms or the legs, or difficulty walking and balancing — suggests spinal cord compression (cervical myelopathy), which needs urgent assessment
New problems with bladder or bowel control alongside neck or arm symptoms
Rapidly progressive or severe weakness in the arm or hand
Neck or arm pain with fever, unexplained weight loss, or a history of cancer — red flags for infection or tumor
Severe neck pain following significant trauma, or neck pain with new neurological deficit after injury

causes.description: Protrusion or extrusion of intervertebral disc material that compresses an adjacent nerve root, and also causes chemical irritation through inflammatory mediators released from the disc. It is the most common cause in younger and middle-aged adults and often follows no specific injury.

Age-related degeneration of the discs, facet joints, and vertebral bodies produces bony spurs (osteophytes), loss of disc height, and narrowing of the intervertebral foramen. This degenerative narrowing compresses the exiting nerve root and is the most common cause in older adults.

In many people, particularly in middle and older age, a combination of a degenerative disc bulge and bony osteophyte together narrows the foramen and compresses the root — a 'hard' and 'soft' compression in combination.

Occupations and activities involving prolonged neck flexion, repetitive neck movement, vibration, or heavy lifting, and sustained poor posture, contribute to disc degeneration and can precipitate or worsen radiculopathy, though they are contributory rather than sole causes.
Acute neck injury, including whiplash and sporting injuries, can cause or unmask cervical radiculopathy through acute disc herniation or by aggravating pre-existing degenerative narrowing.

Spinal tumors, infection (such as epidural abscess or osteomyelitis), inflammatory arthritis affecting the cervical spine, and rarely vascular lesions can compress or irritate a cervical nerve root and must be considered, especially when red-flag features are present.

riskFactors.description: Incidence peaks in the fifth and sixth decades, when degenerative changes in the cervical spine are common while activity levels remain high.
Age-related disc and joint degeneration narrows the intervertebral foramen and is the leading structural risk factor, especially in older adults.
Jobs involving prolonged neck flexion, repetitive neck movements, overhead work, heavy lifting, or whole-body vibration are associated with higher rates of cervical disc disease and radiculopathy.
Smoking accelerates intervertebral disc degeneration and is associated with a higher risk of disc-related spinal problems, including cervical radiculopathy.
A history of significant neck injury, including whiplash, increases the risk of later disc herniation and degenerative change at the affected levels.

riskFactors.factor: Heavy manual work and contact sports
riskFactors.description: Activities that load the cervical spine repetitively or expose it to impact increase the risk of acute disc herniation and accelerated degeneration.
Cervical radiculopathy is slightly more common in men than in women in population studies.

affectedDemographics: Cervical radiculopathy has an annual incidence of around 83 per 100,000 people, making it one of the common causes of neck-and-arm pain.
It peaks in the fifth and sixth decades of life and is slightly more common in men.
Disc herniation tends to cause radiculopathy in younger and middle-aged adults, while degenerative foraminal stenosis predominates in older adults.
The C7 nerve root is the most commonly affected, followed by C6; together these account for the large majority of cases.
Manual workers, those in occupations involving sustained neck postures or vibration, and people who smoke have higher rates.
diagnosisOverview: The diagnosis of cervical radiculopathy is primarily clinical, based on a characteristic history and a focused neurological examination, with imaging and electrodiagnostic studies used to confirm the level, clarify the cause, and guide treatment when needed. The history identifies pain radiating from the neck into the arm in a pattern suggesting a specific nerve root, with sensory and motor features and aggravating positions. The physical examination tests the sensory distribution, muscle strength, and tendon reflexes of each cervical root to localize the lesion, and includes provocative maneuvers: the Spurling test (extension, rotation, and lateral flexion of the neck toward the affected side, which reproduces arm symptoms when positive) is specific for cervical radiculopathy, while the shoulder abduction relief sign and the upper limb tension test add information. The examination must also screen for cervical myelopathy — checking for hyperreflexia, gait disturbance, balance problems, and signs such as Hoffmann's sign — because cord compression changes management. For most people with a clear clinical picture and no red flags, imaging is not needed initially, since the natural history is favourable. When symptoms persist beyond four to six weeks, are severe, are accompanied by significant or progressive weakness, or there are red-flag features, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) of the cervical spine is the investigation of choice, showing disc herniation, foraminal stenosis, and any cord compression; CT or CT myelography is used when MRI is contraindicated or to better define bony anatomy before surgery. Electrodiagnostic studies (nerve conduction studies and electromyography) help confirm the affected root, assess the severity of nerve involvement, and distinguish radiculopathy from peripheral nerve disorders such as carpal tunnel syndrome, ulnar neuropathy, or brachial plexopathy when the picture is unclear. A central diagnostic principle is to correlate imaging findings with the clinical picture, because degenerative changes are extremely common on imaging in people without symptoms, and treating an incidental finding rather than the clinically relevant lesion leads to harm.

diagnosticTests.test: Clinical history and focused neurological examination
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the radicular pattern and localizes the affected nerve root through testing of sensation, strength, and reflexes
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A consultation reviewing the pain pattern and aggravating factors, followed by a structured examination of arm sensation, muscle power, and tendon reflexes; takes 20-30 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Provocative maneuvers (Spurling test, shoulder abduction relief sign)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Reproduce or relieve radicular symptoms to support the diagnosis of cervical radiculopathy
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Bedside tests in which the clinician positions the neck or arm in specific ways; the Spurling test reproduces arm symptoms when positive and is fairly specific.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects signs of spinal cord compression, which is more serious and changes management
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Checks for brisk reflexes, Hoffmann's sign, gait and balance disturbance, and lower-limb signs during the examination.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The imaging investigation of choice when symptoms persist, are severe, or there are red flags — shows disc herniation, foraminal stenosis, and any spinal cord compression
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A scan of 30-45 minutes lying still; no radiation. Findings must be correlated with the clinical picture because degenerative changes are common in people without symptoms.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Used when MRI is contraindicated, or to define bony anatomy and osteophytes before surgery
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A CT scan, sometimes with contrast injected into the spinal canal (myelography); provides detailed bony detail.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirm the affected nerve root, grade the severity of nerve involvement, and distinguish radiculopathy from peripheral nerve disorders such as carpal tunnel syndrome
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Small electrical stimuli and a fine needle electrode are used to test nerve and muscle function; the test takes 45-60 minutes and causes brief discomfort.

treatmentOverview: The treatment of cervical radiculopathy is guided by its favourable natural history: most people improve substantially without surgery, so initial management is conservative and structured, with surgery reserved for specific indications. In the acute phase, treatment focuses on controlling pain and maintaining function: relative rest from aggravating activities (not prolonged immobilization), simple analgesia with paracetamol and non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, a short course of stronger analgesia or a brief tapering course of oral corticosteroids in severe cases, and neuropathic pain agents such as gabapentin, pregabalin, amitriptyline, or duloxetine when there is a prominent burning or electrical neuropathic component. A soft collar may be used briefly for comfort but prolonged collar use is avoided because it weakens the neck muscles. As acute pain settles, physiotherapy becomes central: a graded program of neck and shoulder-girdle strengthening, postural correction, range-of-motion work, manual therapy, and sometimes intermittent cervical traction, along with education and activity modification, improves pain and function and supports return to normal activity. Most people managed this way recover meaningfully within 4-12 weeks. For persistent radicular pain that has not responded to several weeks of good conservative care, fluoroscopically guided cervical epidural or transforaminal corticosteroid injection can reduce pain and improve function in selected patients and may avoid or delay surgery, though it carries small but recognized risks. Surgery is indicated when there is persistent, disabling radicular pain despite 6-12 weeks of appropriate conservative treatment, significant or progressive motor weakness, or evidence of cervical myelopathy (which changes the picture and often warrants earlier surgery). The main operations are anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF), in which the offending disc is removed and the level fused, and cervical disc arthroplasty (artificial disc replacement), which preserves motion at the operated level; posterior foraminotomy is an alternative for certain laterally placed lesions. Surgical outcomes for relieving arm pain and arm weakness are good in well-selected patients. Throughout, the emphasis is on matching the intensity of treatment to the severity and persistence of symptoms, watching for the red flags that demand earlier action, and supporting the person to stay active and return to work.

medicalTreatments.name: Simple analgesia (paracetamol, NSAIDs)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line pain relief for acute cervical radiculopathy. NSAIDs also address the inflammatory component of nerve root irritation; they are used at the lowest effective dose with attention to gastrointestinal, renal, and cardiovascular risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Provides adequate pain control for many patients in the acute phase while the natural history takes its course

medicalTreatments.name: Neuropathic pain agents (gabapentin, pregabalin, amitriptyline, duloxetine)
medicalTreatments.description: Used when there is a prominent burning, shooting, or electrical neuropathic quality to the radicular pain. Titrated to balance benefit against sedation and other side effects.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Helpful for the neuropathic component of radicular pain in many patients, though evidence for radiculopathy specifically is modest

medicalTreatments.name: Short tapering course of oral corticosteroids
medicalTreatments.description: A brief course of oral corticosteroids is used by some clinicians for severe acute radicular pain to reduce nerve root inflammation. Used selectively and short-term because of side effects.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: May provide short-term pain relief in severe acute radiculopathy; evidence is limited and use is individualized

medicalTreatments.name: Structured physiotherapy and exercise
medicalTreatments.description: A graded program of neck and shoulder-girdle strengthening, postural correction, range-of-motion exercises, manual therapy, and education. The cornerstone of conservative management once acute pain begins to settle.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Improves pain, function, and return to activity; most patients recover meaningfully with conservative care within weeks to a few months

medicalTreatments.name: Intermittent cervical traction
medicalTreatments.description: Mechanical or manual traction applied intermittently, often as part of physiotherapy, to reduce pressure on the nerve root. Used selectively in patients who find it helpful.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Provides symptomatic benefit for some patients as an adjunct within a physiotherapy program

medicalTreatments.name: Fluoroscopically guided cervical epidural or transforaminal corticosteroid injection
medicalTreatments.description: Image-guided injection of corticosteroid around the affected nerve root for persistent radicular pain not responding to several weeks of conservative care. Performed by trained proceduralists with attention to the recognized risks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces radicular pain and improves function in selected patients and may delay or avoid surgery; benefit is often temporary and not universal

medicalTreatments.name: Activity modification and short-term soft collar use
medicalTreatments.description: Avoiding aggravating positions and activities, ergonomic adjustment, and brief soft-collar use for comfort. Prolonged collar use and complete inactivity are avoided as they weaken the neck and slow recovery.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Supports symptom control and recovery when combined with a return to graded activity

surgicalOptions.name: Anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF)
surgicalOptions.description: The most common operation for cervical radiculopathy. The offending disc is removed through a small incision at the front of the neck, the nerve root is decompressed, and the level is fused with a graft and usually a plate. Indicated for persistent disabling radiculopathy, significant or progressive weakness, or myelopathy.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Relief of arm pain in roughly 90% of well-selected patients; high rates of fusion and good long-term outcomes, with adjacent-level degeneration a recognized later issue

surgicalOptions.name: Cervical disc arthroplasty (artificial disc replacement)
surgicalOptions.description: An alternative to fusion in suitable patients, in which the diseased disc is replaced with a motion-preserving artificial disc, aiming to maintain movement at the operated level and potentially reduce stress on adjacent levels.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Outcomes for arm pain relief comparable to ACDF in trials, with the theoretical advantage of preserved motion; long-term data continue to accumulate

surgicalOptions.name: Posterior cervical foraminotomy
surgicalOptions.description: A motion-preserving operation performed from the back of the neck that enlarges the intervertebral foramen to decompress the nerve root. Particularly suited to laterally placed soft disc herniations or foraminal osteophytes.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Good relief of radicular symptoms in appropriately selected patients, with the advantage of avoiding fusion

alternativeTreatments.name: Manual therapy and acupuncture
alternativeTreatments.description: Manual therapy techniques delivered by trained physiotherapists, and acupuncture, may provide short-term symptomatic relief for some patients as adjuncts within an active rehabilitation program, though they are not stand-alone cures.

alternativeTreatments.name: Ergonomic and postural intervention
alternativeTreatments.description: Workplace and home ergonomic assessment, adjustment of screen and desk setup, and posture education reduce mechanical strain on the cervical spine and support recovery and prevention of recurrence.

whySeeSpecialist: Most cervical radiculopathy can be managed in primary care with analgesia and physiotherapy, given its favourable natural history. Referral to physical medicine and rehabilitation, neurology, pain medicine, or spinal surgery is warranted when symptoms persist beyond 6-12 weeks despite good conservative care, when there is significant or progressive weakness, when red-flag features are present, or when there are any signs of cervical myelopathy, which is more serious and may need earlier surgical assessment.
doctorSelectionGuide: For conservative care, choose a physiotherapist or physical medicine and rehabilitation service experienced in cervical spine disorders. If surgery is being considered, seek a spinal surgeon — orthopedic or neurosurgical — who performs cervical decompression regularly and can discuss the trade-offs between fusion, disc replacement, and posterior foraminotomy. Any suspicion of cervical myelopathy warrants prompt specialist spinal assessment.
hospitalCriteria: Access to MRI of the cervical spine
Physical medicine and rehabilitation and physiotherapy services experienced in cervical spine care
Spinal surgery service for decompression and fusion or disc replacement when indicated
keyFacilities: Pain medicine and image-guided injection services
preventionStrategies: Maintain good neck posture during desk work, screen use, and driving, with the screen at eye level and frequent position changes.
Take regular breaks from sustained neck flexion and repetitive neck movements at work.
Strengthen the neck and shoulder-girdle muscles with regular exercise to support the cervical spine.
Use correct lifting technique and avoid carrying heavy loads in ways that strain the neck.
Stop smoking, which accelerates degeneration of the intervertebral discs.
Set up workstations and vehicle seats ergonomically to reduce sustained loading of the cervical spine.
Use appropriate protective technique and conditioning in contact sports to reduce neck injury risk.
lifestyleModifications: Stay active and continue gentle movement rather than resting the neck completely, which weakens the supporting muscles.
Modify aggravating activities and positions in the short term while symptoms settle, then gradually return to full activity.
Improve workstation ergonomics — screen at eye level, supportive chair, and regular position changes.
Do prescribed neck and shoulder-girdle exercises consistently to build support and reduce recurrence.
Use a supportive pillow that maintains a neutral neck position during sleep.
Stop smoking to slow disc degeneration.
Manage pain proactively so that fear of movement does not lead to deconditioning.

recommended: A balanced diet supporting a healthy body weight to reduce overall spinal load
Adequate protein to support neck and shoulder-girdle muscle strengthening
Calcium- and vitamin-D-rich foods for general bone and spine health
An anti-inflammatory dietary pattern (vegetables, fruit, fish, whole grains, olive oil)
avoid: Excess alcohol, which can interact with neuropathic pain medications and disturb sleep
Highly processed, high-sugar diets associated with weight gain and worse musculoskeletal pain outcomes
Long periods of inactivity that lead to deconditioning of the neck and shoulder muscles
Reliance on dietary supplements as a substitute for evidence-based exercise and rehabilitation

exerciseGuidelines: Once acute pain begins to settle, a graded exercise program guided by a physiotherapist is the foundation of recovery: neck and shoulder-girdle strengthening, postural correction, range-of-motion work, and a gradual return to normal aerobic activity. Avoid heavy loading and provocative neck positions in the early phase, but do not avoid movement altogether, as prolonged rest weakens the supporting muscles and slows recovery. Most people can progressively return to full activity and sport as symptoms and strength improve.
dailyManagement: Do prescribed neck and shoulder-girdle exercises every day as directed by the physiotherapist.
Take analgesia regularly enough in the acute phase to stay active and sleep, then taper as symptoms settle.
Maintain good posture and an ergonomic workstation, with frequent position changes.
Use a supportive pillow and a comfortable neutral neck position for sleep.
Modify aggravating activities temporarily, then progressively return to normal activity.
Monitor for any new or worsening weakness, balance problems, or bladder or bowel symptoms and seek prompt review if they occur.
prognosis: Cervical radiculopathy has a favourable natural history, and this shapes its prognosis and management. The majority of people — commonly cited as around three-quarters or more — improve substantially with conservative treatment within weeks to a few months, and many recover fully. Disc herniation-related radiculopathy in particular has a good outlook, as herniated disc material frequently resorbs over time; spondylotic radiculopathy from fixed bony narrowing can be more persistent. Pain usually improves before any motor weakness fully recovers, and mild weakness often resolves with time, though significant or long-standing weakness recovers less reliably. A minority of people have persistent or recurrent symptoms despite good conservative care, and it is this group, together with those who have significant or progressive weakness or signs of cervical myelopathy, for whom surgery is considered. Surgical outcomes in well-selected patients are good: anterior cervical discectomy and fusion and cervical disc arthroplasty relieve arm pain in around 90% of appropriately chosen patients, and arm weakness improves in most. Recognized longer-term considerations after fusion include adjacent-level degeneration over years. Overall, the key prognostic messages are that most people do well without surgery, that early reassurance and a structured active rehabilitation approach support recovery, and that the small number with red-flag features or true myelopathy need to be identified promptly because their management and urgency are different.
recoveryTimeline: Acute radicular pain often begins to settle within the first one to three weeks with analgesia and relative rest. Structured physiotherapy over the following weeks supports recovery, and most people improve substantially within 4-12 weeks. Sensory symptoms and mild weakness may take longer — weeks to several months — to resolve. If significant symptoms persist beyond 6-12 weeks of good conservative care, injection or surgery is considered. After surgery, arm pain relief is often rapid, with return to most activities over 6-12 weeks depending on the procedure and occupation.
complications: Persistent or recurrent radicular pain despite conservative treatment.
Incomplete recovery of motor weakness when nerve root compression is significant or prolonged.
Chronic neck pain and reduced neck function.
Deconditioning of the neck and shoulder muscles from prolonged inactivity or excessive collar use.
Progression to or unrecognized coexisting cervical myelopathy from spinal cord compression.
Side effects of medications, including gastrointestinal effects of NSAIDs and sedation from neuropathic agents.
Surgical complications — including, uncommonly, hoarseness or swallowing difficulty after anterior surgery, non-union of a fusion, and adjacent-level degeneration over the longer term.
Reduced work capacity and quality of life when symptoms are persistent.

supportResources.name: North American Spine Society (NASS)
supportResources.description: Professional body publishing the clinical guideline on cervical radiculopathy and providing patient education resources.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Orthopaedic Surgeons - OrthoInfo
supportResources.description: Patient-facing information on cervical radiculopathy (pinched nerve in the neck), its treatment, and recovery.

supportResources.name: American Association of Neurological Surgeons
supportResources.description: Patient resources on cervical spine conditions, including radiculopathy and the surgical options.

supportResources.name: Versus Arthritis (UK)
supportResources.description: UK charity with patient information on neck pain, nerve-related arm pain, and self-management.

confusedWithConditions.name: Carpal Tunnel Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Carpal tunnel syndrome compresses the median nerve at the wrist, causing numbness and tingling in the thumb, index, and middle fingers, often worse at night, without neck pain. Cervical radiculopathy causes symptoms that radiate from the neck in a nerve-root pattern, may include neck pain, and changes with neck position. Nerve conduction studies distinguish them, and the two can coexist.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lumbar Radiculopathy
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lumbar radiculopathy is the same disease process — a compressed or irritated nerve root — but in the lower back, causing radiating leg pain (sciatica) rather than arm pain. The diagnostic approach and conservative-first management principles are similar, but the affected region and the specific nerve roots differ.

confusedWithConditions.name: Neuropathic Pain
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Neuropathic pain is the broad category of pain from nerve damage or dysfunction; cervical radiculopathy is one specific structural cause of it. Cervical radiculopathy has an identifiable compressed nerve root, a root-specific pattern, and often a structural cause treatable with conservative care, injection, or surgery.

confusedWithConditions.name: Back Pain
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Mechanical neck pain (often grouped with back pain) is pain localized to the neck and surrounding muscles without nerve root involvement, so it lacks the radiating arm pain, dermatomal numbness, specific weakness, and reflex changes that define cervical radiculopathy.

relatedConditions.name: Lumbar Radiculopathy
relatedConditions.relevance: The same disease process affecting nerve roots in the lower back; shares diagnostic approach and conservative-first management.

relatedConditions.name: Chronic Low Back Pain
relatedConditions.relevance: A common coexisting spinal condition; degenerative spine disease often affects multiple regions.

relatedConditions.name: Back Pain
relatedConditions.relevance: Mechanical neck and back pain frequently coexists with and must be distinguished from radiculopathy.

relatedConditions.name: Neuropathic Pain
relatedConditions.relevance: Cervical radiculopathy is a structural cause of neuropathic pain and shares some pharmacological treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Carpal Tunnel Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: A common peripheral cause of hand symptoms that must be distinguished from, and can coexist with, cervical radiculopathy.

faqs.question: What is cervical radiculopathy?
faqs.answer: Cervical radiculopathy is the set of symptoms — pain, numbness, tingling, and weakness — that occurs when a nerve root in the neck is compressed or irritated. The symptoms radiate from the neck into the shoulder, arm, and hand in a pattern that follows the affected nerve root. It is often called a pinched nerve in the neck.

faqs.question: What causes cervical radiculopathy?
faqs.answer: The two main causes are a herniated cervical disc, more common in younger and middle-aged adults, and degenerative narrowing of the bony channel the nerve passes through (foraminal stenosis from spondylosis), more common in older adults. Less common causes include trauma, tumors, and infection.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of cervical radiculopathy?
faqs.answer: Symptoms include pain radiating from the neck into the arm and hand, numbness and tingling in specific fingers, and weakness in particular arm or hand muscles. Symptoms often change with neck position and may be eased by resting the hand on top of the head. Neck pain may or may not be prominent.

faqs.question: Does cervical radiculopathy go away on its own?
faqs.answer: In most cases, yes. Cervical radiculopathy has a favourable natural history — the majority of people improve substantially with conservative treatment such as analgesia and physiotherapy within weeks to a few months, and many recover fully. Surgery is needed only for a minority.

faqs.question: How is cervical radiculopathy diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis is mainly clinical, based on the pattern of symptoms and a neurological examination of arm sensation, strength, and reflexes, supported by provocative tests such as the Spurling test. MRI of the neck is used when symptoms persist or there are red flags, and nerve conduction studies help distinguish it from other nerve problems.

faqs.question: How is cervical radiculopathy treated?
faqs.answer: Most cases are treated without surgery: relative rest from aggravating activities, analgesia including NSAIDs, neuropathic pain medication if needed, and a structured physiotherapy program. Image-guided steroid injections help some people with persistent pain, and surgery is reserved for specific indications.

faqs.question: When does cervical radiculopathy need surgery?
faqs.answer: Surgery is considered when there is persistent disabling arm pain despite 6-12 weeks of good conservative treatment, significant or progressive muscle weakness, or evidence of spinal cord compression (cervical myelopathy). Most people do not need surgery.

faqs.question: Which nerve roots are most often affected?
faqs.answer: The C7 nerve root is the most commonly affected, followed by C6. Each root produces a characteristic pattern: C7 radiculopathy affects the middle finger and elbow extension, while C6 radiculopathy affects the thumb and index finger and elbow flexion.

faqs.question: What is the difference between cervical radiculopathy and cervical myelopathy?
faqs.answer: Cervical radiculopathy is compression of a nerve root, causing symptoms in one arm. Cervical myelopathy is compression of the spinal cord itself, which is more serious and can cause problems in both arms and the legs, clumsiness, balance and walking difficulty, and bladder symptoms. Myelopathy often needs earlier surgical assessment.

faqs.question: How long does cervical radiculopathy take to recover?
faqs.answer: Acute pain often begins to settle within one to three weeks, and most people improve substantially within 4-12 weeks with conservative treatment. Numbness and mild weakness can take longer, sometimes several months. Persistent symptoms beyond 6-12 weeks may prompt consideration of injection or surgery.

faqs.question: Is cervical radiculopathy the same as a pinched nerve?
faqs.answer: Yes. 'Pinched nerve in the neck' is the common term for cervical radiculopathy. It describes a nerve root being compressed or irritated as it exits the spine, producing the characteristic radiating pain, numbness, tingling, and weakness in the arm.

faqs.question: Can cervical radiculopathy cause permanent weakness?
faqs.answer: Most weakness improves with time, especially when it is mild. However, significant or long-standing weakness from prolonged nerve root compression recovers less reliably. This is one reason significant or progressive weakness is an indication to consider earlier surgical treatment.

faqs.question: What is the Spurling test?
faqs.answer: The Spurling test is a bedside examination maneuver in which the clinician extends, rotates, and laterally flexes the neck toward the affected side. If this reproduces the radiating arm symptoms, the test is positive and supports the diagnosis of cervical radiculopathy.

faqs.question: Should I rest or stay active with cervical radiculopathy?
faqs.answer: Relative rest from specific aggravating activities is helpful in the acute phase, but complete inactivity and prolonged collar use are discouraged because they weaken the neck and slow recovery. Staying generally active and progressing into a graded physiotherapy program supports better recovery.

faqs.question: Do steroid injections help cervical radiculopathy?
faqs.answer: Image-guided cervical epidural or transforaminal steroid injections can reduce radicular pain and improve function in selected patients whose symptoms have not responded to several weeks of conservative care, and may delay or avoid surgery. Benefit is often temporary and not universal, and there are small recognized risks.

faqs.question: What is the difference between cervical radiculopathy and carpal tunnel syndrome?
faqs.answer: Cervical radiculopathy comes from a compressed nerve root in the neck and causes symptoms that radiate from the neck in a root-specific pattern. Carpal tunnel syndrome comes from compression of the median nerve at the wrist, causing numbness in the thumb and adjacent fingers, often at night, without neck pain. Nerve conduction studies distinguish them, and they can coexist.

faqs.question: Can poor posture cause cervical radiculopathy?
faqs.answer: Sustained poor posture and prolonged neck flexion contribute to disc degeneration and can precipitate or worsen cervical radiculopathy, but they are contributory rather than the sole cause. Improving posture and workstation ergonomics is part of both treatment and prevention.

faqs.question: What surgery is used for cervical radiculopathy?
faqs.answer: The main operations are anterior cervical discectomy and fusion (ACDF), which removes the offending disc and fuses the level, and cervical disc arthroplasty (artificial disc replacement), which preserves motion. Posterior cervical foraminotomy is an alternative for certain laterally placed lesions. Arm pain relief is good in around 90% of well-selected patients.

faqs.question: When should I seek urgent medical attention for neck and arm symptoms?
faqs.answer: Seek urgent assessment if you develop weakness or numbness in both arms or the legs, difficulty walking or with balance, new bladder or bowel problems, rapidly progressive weakness, or neck and arm pain with fever, unexplained weight loss, or a history of cancer. These suggest spinal cord compression or another serious cause.

faqs.question: Can cervical radiculopathy come back?
faqs.answer: Yes, recurrence is possible, either at the same level or a different level, particularly when there is underlying degenerative spine disease. Maintaining neck and shoulder-girdle strength, good posture and ergonomics, not smoking, and continuing prescribed exercises reduce the risk of recurrence.

simpleName: Cervical radiculopathy (pinched nerve in the neck)
symptomKeywords: pain relieved by hand on head
metaDescription: Cervical radiculopathy (pinched nerve in the neck): radiating arm pain, numbness and weakness. Causes, diagnosis, physiotherapy and surgery reviewed.
